Postnatal Care and Life Expectancy: A Global Perspective

Author

Adetayo Eden David

An exploratory data story using UNICEF indicators to examine global inequalities in postnatal care and population health outcomes.

Introduction

The first 48 hours after childbirth represent one of the most critical periods for maternal and newborn health. Complications during this window account for a significant share of preventable maternal and neonatal deaths worldwide. Despite this, access to timely postnatal care remains highly uneven across countries, reflecting broader inequalities in health system capacity, infrastructure, and access to essential services.

This data story uses UNICEF indicators to examine global patterns in postnatal care coverage and explores how access to postnatal care aligns with life expectancy as a broader measure of population health.

Global Challenge (Why This Matters)

Across the world, mothers do not have the same chance of receiving essential care after birth. The first 48 hours are a high‑risk window for complications, yet access to postnatal care remains uneven across countries. This inequality is not only a maternal health issue — it reflects broader health system capacity and can be linked to long‑term population health outcomes.

What This Report Examines

This report uses UNICEF postnatal care indicator data and country‑level metadata to answer two questions:

  1. How does access to postnatal care within 48 hours vary globally?
  2. How is postnatal care coverage associated with life expectancy at birth?

Analysis Notation: Life expectancy is not a direct outcome of postnatal care alone, but as a summary measure of population health it provides useful context for understanding how countries with stronger maternal health systems often achieve better long‑term outcomes.

Headline Facts (From The Data)

  • Postnatal care coverage (latest year per country) is available for 112 countries.
  • Latest postnatal care values range from 5.0% to 99.8%.
  • When we align the latest postnatal care year with metadata, 108 countries also have life expectancy in the same year.
  • In that aligned set, life expectancy ranges from 31.5 to 80.1 years.

Why Postnatal Care Matters

The first two days after birth are critical. Postnatal care within 48 hours can identify complications, support recovery, and connect mothers to follow-up services. If access is limited, preventable complications can go unnoticed.

What We Expect To See

If a country has stronger health system access overall, we might expect both higher postnatal care coverage and higher life expectancy. The charts below show where postnatal care is highest and lowest globally, and then examine its relationship with life expectancy.

Global Distribution of Postnatal Care (%)

The global distribution of postnatal care coverage shows substantial regional inequality. Many countries in sub‑Saharan Africa and parts of South Asia exhibit markedly lower coverage compared to Europe and the Americas. These patterns reflect differences in health system capacity and access to care.

Lowest Postnatal Care Coverage Countries

The bar chart highlights countries with the lowest reported postnatal care coverage. These countries are often characterised by limited healthcare infrastructure, workforce shortages, and barriers to accessing care, particularly in rural or fragile settings. The concentration of low coverage underscores the need for targeted maternal health interventions.

The Relationship Between Postnatal Care vs Life Expectancy

The scatterplot reveals a positive association between postnatal care coverage and life expectancy. Countries with higher access to postnatal care tend to display longer average life expectancy. While this relationship does not imply causation, it is consistent with evidence that stronger maternal health systems align with better overall population health outcomes.

Postnatal Care Coverage Over Time

The time series illustrates divergent trends in postnatal care coverage across countries. While some countries demonstrate steady improvements over time, others show stagnation or slow progress. These differences suggest that sustained policy commitment and long‑term investment are critical for expanding postnatal care access.

Call to Action

Expanding access to postnatal care within the first 48 hours after birth represents a practical and scalable opportunity to improve maternal and newborn health outcomes globally. The patterns observed in this analysis suggest that countries with higher postnatal care coverage also tend to achieve better population health outcomes. Policymakers and development partners should prioritise sustained investment in frontline maternal health services, particularly in countries identified as having persistently low coverage. Strengthening postnatal care delivery has the potential to contribute to healthier life trajectories across generations.

Limitations and Next Steps

  • National averages can hide sub-national inequalities.
  • The charts show association, not causation.
  • The life expectancy join uses the same year as each country’s latest postnatal care observation; countries missing that metadata-year are excluded from the scatterplot.
